text: ESTERHA'Z, a village of Hungary, in the com. and 15 m. ESE of Oedenburg, on the S shore of the Neusiedler lake. Pop. 375. There is a fine palace here, formerly the principal seat of the princely house of Esterhazy. It is built in the Versailles style, and enormous sums have been expended upon it; but it has long been uninhabited; the only part in use being the stables. The chief seat of administration for the Esterhazy estates, which are said to equal the kingdom of Wurtemberg in size, and to contain 40 towns, 130 villages, and 34 castles, is at Eisenstadt, at the foot of the Leitha mountains.

ESTERNAY, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Epernay.— The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 7,797 in 1841.—The com. is 31 m. SW of Epernay, on the Morin river. Pop. 947. It has a porcelain manufactory.

ESTERO REAL, a river of San Salvador, which flows W, and falls into the SE point of the gulf of Fonseca, in N lat. 12° 48′, 30 m. NNW of Realejo. It has been proposed to make the mouth of this river, instead of Port Realejo, the mouth of the Nicaraguan ship-canal. It appears that the direct length of a canal leading NW from Lake Managua to the Estero Real would not exceed the distance from that lake to Realejo, viz., 45 m.; and that the highest elevation to be overcome would be only 50 ft., while between the lake and Realejo there is a rise of 73 ft. Another presumed advantage of the Estero line is that while the harbour of Realejo, though secure, is very small, the gulf of Fonseca would "float the navies of the world." See Fonseca.
ESTERO-DEL-PURGATORIO, a small island in the Pacific, near the coast of Chili, in S lat. 46° 30'.

ESTERO (SANTIAGO DEL), a town in the Buenos- Ayrean prov. of Tucuman, 226 m. S of Salta, on the great road from that city to Buenos-Ayres, in S lat. 27° 47', upon the banks of a river which, under the name of the Rio Dolcè, runs S to the great lakes called the Poroagos, to the W of Santa Fé. It is an illbuilt place, containing not more than 4,000 souls.
ESTEROS (Los), a bay on the W coast of New California, between Punta-del-Esteros and Mount
ESTEROS (Punta del), a cape on the W coast | of New California, in N lat. 35° 30'.
ESTERQUEL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 57 m. SSE of Saragossa, near the r. bank of the San Martin.
ESTERRIDANEO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Lerida, 27 m. NW of Urgel.
ESTEVAN. See ESTEBAN.
ESTEVAN (PORT SAN), a harbour on the W coast of Patagonia, under Rescue-point, 25 m. S by E of Cape Taytao.
ESTEVAN (SAN), an island in the Northern ocean, off the W coast of New Britain, in N lat. 53°, to the S of Banks's island.

ESTEVAO (SIERRA-DE-SANTO), a chain of mountains in the Brazilian prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Villa-Rica, stretching between the parallels of 20° and 21° S, in a direction from NE to SW. It separates the basin of the Manhuazu from that of the Cuvaté.

ESTEY-DE-CASTRES, or Gue'mort, a river of France, in the dep. of La Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux, rising near Saint Magne, and running NE, past Castres, to the Garonne, which it joins on the l. bank, after a course of 21 m.
ESTHER ISLAND, an island in Prince William's sound, near the W coast of North America, in N lat. 60° 51'. It is about 24 m. in circumf.

ESTHONIA, a country of European Russia, adjacent to Livonia, and extending along the S side of the gulf of Finland. It now forms the gov. of REVEL, which see. E. belonged in early times to the Teutonic knights; and was afterwards an object of contest between Sweden, Russia, and Poland. The treaty of Oliva, in 1660, gave it to Sweden; that of Nystad, in 1721, transferred it to Russia, in whose possession it has ever since remained.
ESTILL, a co. of Kentucky, U. S., situated centrally towards the E part of the state, and watered by Kentucky river. Area 864 sq. m. Pop. 5,535. The cap. is Irvine.
ESTILLVILLE, a town in Scott co., in the state of Virginia, on Mocassin creek, 344 m. W by S of Richmond.
ESTINNES-AU-MONT, a commune and village of Belgium, in Hainault, 20 m. W of Charleroi. Pop. 1,576. It has some linen thread manufactories.
ESTINNES-AU-VAL, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 8 m. E of Mons. Pop. 733.
ESTISSAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aube, 17 m. W of Troyes. Pop. 1,537. The cant. comprises 10 com., and had a pop. of 6,899 in 1841.
ESTIVAO-DE-CACHOVE, a small town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, 11 m. N of Loule.
ESTIVAREILLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Bonnet, 5 m. N of Montlucon. Pop. 1,565.
ESTOPINAN, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Huesca, 21 m. E of Barbastro.
ESTOVAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Correze, cant. of Vigeors. Pop. 844.
ESTRABLEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Vienne. Pop. 1,228.
ESTRECHY, a town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, 5 m. N of Etampes. Pop. 1,014.
ESTRE'ES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Le Catelet. Pop. 1,116. -Also a commune and village in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Arleux, Pop. 1,018.
ESTRE'ES - DE'NIE'COURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Chaulnes. Pop. 604. EST France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Peronne. Pop. 1,118.

ESTRE'ES-SAINT-DENIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Compiegne.—The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. of 10,674 in 1841.—The com. is 8 m. W of Compiegne. Pop. 1,308. It has rope-works, and beet-root sugar manufactories.

ESTRELLA, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. of Antioquia, in N lat. 6° 11', at an alt. of 5,800 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 628.—Also a river of Costa-Rica, flowing into the Pacific, in N lat. 9° 7', after a course of about 60 m. in a S and SW direction.

ESTRELLA (PORTO DE), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, situated in the angle of the confluence of the Saracurana with the Inhumirim. It is the common harbour between Rio-de-Janeiro and the prov. of Minas-Geraes. Raw cotton, coarse cotton-stuffs, cheese, bacon, quince marmalade, gold dust, and precious stones are brought here from the interior; and the greater part of the goods which are sent from Rio to Minas, Goyaz, and Matto-Grosso, pass through this place. The means of transport are long trains of mules. Boats set out daily from Rio between 11 and 12 o'clock, as soon as the seabreeze springs up, and arrive at E. in the evening. An expansive paved road leads from Piedade, 4 m. NE of E., to the summit of the Serra-de-Estrella, which it crosses at an alt. of 4,000 ft. above sea-level. The serra has a length of 21 m.; and is connected at its E extremity with the Serra-dos-Orgãos.

ESTRELLA (SERRA-DA), a lofty mountain-range in Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, extending from the frontiers of Estremadura, E to the Spanish frontier, where it is connected with the Sierra-de-Gata. It stretches, like most of the other mountain-ranges in the peninsula, from NE to SW. It rises from a mountain - plain of a considerable height, and is covered with snow during more than four months of the year. Link has estimated its elevation at 5,000 or 6,000 ft. above the level of the sea. The S or highest part of the range is called Serra Brava, or 'the Wild mountains,' from their steep and rocky character; the N and lower part of the range is called Serra Mansa, or 'the Gentle mountains.' The whole of these mountains consist of granite. The highest summit, the Malhao-de-Serra, forms a large but gently arched plain, of so great extent, that the spectator does not at all observe the rough rocky sides that surround the mountain everywhere but to the NE. There are two lakes on these mountains. One called the Lagoa Escura, or Dark lake, of a fine round form, and most romantically situated, being surrounded with high and wild rocks, is deep and cold. The Lagoa Rotonda, which is the smaller, is also surrounded with high rocks, and is remarkable for the transparency of its water. The Lagoa Longa or Comprides, occupies the middle of a valley of considerable length. Its breadth is unequal, and, from its marshy banks, it is less beautiful than the other two. The waters descending from the N flanks of this chain flow to the Duero and the Mondego; those from the S to the Zezere, an affluent of the Tagus.

ESTREMADURA, a prov. of Portugal, bounded by Beira on the N; by Alemtejo and Beira on the S; and by the sea on the W. It extends about 140 m. from N to S, and is about 70 m. in breadth; and contains an area of 7,176 sq. m. It is intersected in the direction of E and W by the Tagus. This prov. forms a long and narrow stripe of land, extending along the sea-shore, from the embouchure of the Mondego to below the town of Setuval. On account of its proximity to the Atlantic, the climate is mild; but be-

ESTRE'ES - EN - CHAUSCES, a commune of tween the end of July and the beginning of September, every thing is parched: not a blade of grass is to be seen, and the evergreens are shrivelled up. The heat continues unintermitting, with a sky almost always serene. The N wind prevails in summer; but its direction being changed by the mountains of Cintra, it becomes a NW, and cools the air considerably. The greatest heat always accompanies the E wind. In the summer of 1798, the temp. was 104°; a heat of 96° is not uncommon.—The soil round Lisbon consists of limestone and basalt. In some places, the limestone is of close texture and excellent for building. The basalt begins not far from the sea, and proceeds through Quelus towards Bellas; a branch of the basaltic mountains stretches beyond Lisbon, and unites with the Sierra-da-Estrella towards Bellas, whence the basalt district extends as far as Cabeça-de-Montachique. The mountains of Cintra consist of granite, composed of clear white quartz, a reddish felspar, and black mica, against which leans a white foliaceous limestone. The S side of these mountains is arid, naked, and parchedup, and consists of bare heaped-up rocks, affording a wild and a dreary prospect. The N side of the range is to a certain height covered with countryhouses, and charming quintas.—The mildness of the climate is peculiarly favourable to agriculture, but the soil is productive or unproductive according to the quantity of rain which falls. Wheat is commonly sown in the neighbourhood of Lisbon; some barley is also sown; but rye is only grown for cattle, and oats are never seen. The fallows are ploughed in autumn, a second time in May, and again at seedtime, when the autumnal rains have softened the earth. Light soils are dug; those which are heavy are ploughed with oxen of extraordinary size, strength, and beauty. The harvest is in May. The corn is generally thrashed, but is sometimes trodden out by oxen and horses. Rye is often in ear in February and March, and is commonly cut down for fodder before it is ripe. Spanish potatoes are occasionally grown; but this root is generally imported from England and Ireland. The principal fruits are citrons, lemons, oranges, figs, dates, and almonds. Great quantities of wine are made in the prov., that of Setuval, Bucellas, and Carcavellos is highly esteemed. Salt forms one of the chief articles of its commerce.—The pop. in 1801 was 678,360; in 1846, 782,875.—The prov. is divided into the districts of Lisbon, Leira, and Santarem; which are subdivided into 136 concelhos. The principal towns are Lisbon, Leira, Abrantes, Pombal, Alcobaça, Thomar, Santarem, Alanques, Torres-Vedras, Cintra, and Cascaes.

ESTREMADURA, the largest and most fertile, though the least populous and least cultivated, of the provs. of Spain. It is bounded on the N and NE by the kingdom of Leon; on the E by New Castile; on the S and SE by the kingdom of Seville; and on the W by the Portuguese provs. of Estremadura, and Alemtejo. It is about 190 m. long from N to S; and 80 m. broad from E to W; and contains 14,166 sq. m.—On the N this prov. is skirted by the Sierras of Grados, Bejar, and Gata; on the S by the Sierra-Constantina. In the centre, the Sierra-de- Guadalupe, and San-Manuel divide the basin of the Tagus from that of the Guadiana.—Link informs us, that before he arrived at Almaraz, he found along the hills to the N of the Tagus slaty granite, on which, nearer the vertex, rested a clay-slate mixed with mica. Towards the Puerto, he found clay-slate with mica, sandstone slate, and green marl; and between these, strata of carbonates, and traces of phosphates, of limestone, which is also found in strata in the mountains farther to the W. The sandstone is less slaty higher up the Puerto; and the summit is III covered with ratchil. Towards Jorayciego, on the other side, the clay-slate and sand-slate continue to the neighbourhood of Truxillo, the country around which is composed wholly of granite. Sand-slate and clay-slate are found near Meajadas; but the ratchil begins about the Venta-del-despoblado, at a village called San-Pedro, extending along the Guadiana to Merida. Several of the E. mountains, particularly that of Guadalupe, are covered with medicinal plants.—The principal rivers are the Tagus and the Guadiana, both of which are navigable. The minor rivers are the Allagon, the Cuyar, the Sabor, the Savar, the Allegrette, the Alamonte, the Guyar, the Navazo, the Naluenga, the Lentrin, the Rivello, the Guadajiera, the Caya, the Mutachel, the Guadarranque, the Gevara, the Albarragena, and the Abrilongo.—The principal mineral springs in the province, are those of Cheles, 9 leagues from Talavera-la-Real; the Fuente-de-las-Aguzaderas, near Zafra, upon the mountain Castellar; the Fuente-dell'-Carrasco, near the village of Almaharrin; the Fuente-de-Bernardo-Estevard, near Barcarotta, a small town about 7 leagues from Badajoz; and another, near Alange, a town 3 leagues to the E of Merida. The first five of these are cold, and the last is thermal.—Although the high mountains are covered with snow towards the end of November, yet the winter is not severe.—The Romans were attracted to this province by the fertility of its soil, and the mildness of its climate; and it was converted into a garden by the industry of the Moors. Upon the expulsion of the Moors, it was greatly depopulated; and since that time it has continued in a state of comparative impoverishment even to Spain. The soil is rich; but it is almost completely reduced to a state of rank pasturage by neglect. Wheat and rye are almost the sole productions; and the quantity grown is nearly sufficient for the support of a scanty pop. of 547,420 in 1833. Olive-trees and vines are not numerous; chestnut-trees are more abundant, and afford nourishment to many of the inhabitants. Every autumn, about 4,000,000 Merino sheep are sent into this prov., and remain during the winter feeding upon the grass of its immense plains. The sheep reared in the prov. are estimated at 700,000. Numerous herds of swine are reared. Gardens and fruit-trees abound between the Pueblade-la-Culzada and Montijo, in the Vera-de-Plasencia. Olive-trees are numerous at Banos; vines at Talavera-la-Vieja and Banos; and numerous plantations of oak, chestnut, and other trees occur round Talavera, in the Vera-de-Plasencia, and near Ervas, Banos, and Bejar. Around Caceres and Plasencia, and in the valley of Bejar, agriculture is best attended to.—During several centuries, this prov. possessed good manufactories of broad cloths, and other woollen stuffs. The principal manufactories which existed before the revolution, were one of hats at Badajoz, two similar manufactories at Zafra, a number of tanyards at Zafra and at Caceres, and a manufactory of broad cloths at Arrago - del - Puerco. There is a manufactory of second cloths at Ervas; and another very considerable one at Bejar still sends a quantity of cloth to Castile and Andalusia. The roads and inns in E. are very wretched. The road which leads into Portugal is the best kept, and was always carefully repaired when any of the royal family of Spain and Portugal travelled that way. Link describes this road as more magnificent than any of the English roads, and better than most of the French.—There are few schools in E., and the inhabitants are in a state of extreme ignorance.- This prov. contains three bishopries, Badajoz, Plasencia, and Coria. It is subdivided into the two provs. of Badajoz and Caceres.—The chief towns are Badajoz, which is the capital, Plasencia, Coria, Merida, Truxillo, Xeros-de-los-Cavalleros, Llerena, Almatana, Zafra, Caceres, Albuquerque, and Olivença.
ESTREMERA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. ESE of Madrid, on the r. bank of the Tagus.
ESTREMIANA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. W of Vittoria.
ESTREMOZ. See Extremos.

ESTREMOZ, a strong town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, situated in an agreeable tract on the Tarra, in N lat. 38° 46', 24 m. NE of Evora, and 80 m. E of Lisbon. It consists of upper and lower towns,—the former standing with the citadel on an eminence, the latter in the valley below. Pop. 6,500. It is one of the chief fortresses in the kingdom; but its works have been neglected since Elvas was rendered nearly impregnable. The surrounding district is fertile, and finely diversified with deep valleys and forest-clad hills.

ESTRONDO (SERRA), a mountain-chain of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, between the parallels of 11° and 13°. It is the N prolongation of the Cordillera-Grande, and has a length of about 90 m. From its E flanks the Carrabrava, the Tabraas, and the Crixa-Pequena, descend to join the Tocantins.
ESUTLA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Oaxaca. Pop. 1,000, chiefly Indians.
ESVRES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, 8 m. SSE of Tours, on the r. bank of the Indre. Pop. 1,739.
ESZEK. See Esseck.

ETABLES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Saint-Brieuc.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1841, 11,850.—The town, 9 m. NNW of Saint- Brieuc, had a pop. of 3,088 in 1841.
ETAK, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Diarbekir, 50 m. NNE of Diarbekir.
ETALLE, a town of Belgium, in the prov. and 26 m. W of Luxemburg, on the Semoy. Pop. 1,449.
ETAGNAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Chabanais. Pop. 1,471.

ETAIN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, arrond. of Verdun-sur-Meuse.— The cant., comprising 29 com., had a pop. of 12,037 in 1841. - The com. and town, situated on the Ornès, 10 m. ENE of Verdun, had a pop. of 2,961 in 1841. There are cotton-factories here, and large tan-works.
ETAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Auxerre, cant. of Coulange-sur-Yonne. Pop. 1,508.
ETALANTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Aignay-le-Duc. Pop. 589.

ETAMPES, an arrondissement, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise.—The arrond. has an area of 80,018 hect., and comprises the 4 cant. of E., La-Ferte-Aleps, Meréville, and Milly. Pop. in 1841, 40,651; in 1846, 41,459.— The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,748; in 1841, 14,426.—The town, which is the cap. of the dep., is situated at the confluence of the Etampes with the Juine, at an alt. of 262 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 48° 26′ 49", E long. 1° 9', 28 m. S by W of Paris, on the Paris and Orleans railroad. It is an ancient town, and contains some curious old houses, a Gothic church of the 13th cent., and the remains of a very ancient castle. It has a considerable trade in flour, wool, corn, and honey, the produce of the surrounding country; besides manufactures of soap, leather, woollen yarn, and hosiery. Pop. in 1789, 6,344; in 1831, 8,109; in 1846, 8,157.—E. suffered greatly in the civil wars of the 14th and 15th cents. It was erected into a duchy by Francis I. In 156" ETH it was taken by the Huguenots; and in 1590 it was taken by Henry IV. from the party of the League.

ETAPLES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Montreuil-sur-Mer.—The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. of 8,751 in 1841.—The town, situated near the mouth of the Canche, has an active coasting-commerce in salt, herrings, beer, and brandy. The tonnage of its port in 1841 amounted to 41 ves- <math>sels = 442 tons.</math>
ETAVES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Bohain. Pop. 1,381.

ETAWEH, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, situated between the rivers Jumna and Ganges, about the 27th parallel of N lat. Area 3,450 sq. m. When a due proportion of rain falls, it is a very productive territory, yielding all kinds of grain except rice, in great abundance; it also produces sugar, tobacco, and cotton. Lemons, limes, oranges, citrons, melons, plantains, mangoes, and guavas, grow luxuriantly; and many European vegetables are successfully cultivated. The ornithology and entomology of the district are both peculiarly rich. In 1801 E. was ceded by the nabob of Oude to the British. It is governed by a judge and collector, subject to the court at Bareilly. Its principal towns are E., Minpuri, Kanoje, and Belah.

ETAWEH, the cap. of the above district, is situated on the E or l. bank of the Jumna, in N lat. 26° 46', 70 m. SE of Agra. It was formerly the residence of a rajah, and a place of much consequence both in trade and war as a chief border-town between the Moguls and Mahrattas. It still carries on some traffic in grain, sugar, and cotton, and there is a manufacture of coarse cotton cloths in the vicinity; but "ruin and desolation appear to have shaken hands with the major part," [Archer,] although there are several picturesque buildings, and a splendid ghaut. It is now the station of the civil establishment of the district, and has a cantonment for a battalion of native infantry, which exhibits in full perfection the dreary features of a jungle-station. The banks of the river are here prodigiously high, and are cut and perforated into enormous holes and ravines by the action of the rains, while the soil is a hard conker or conglomerated earth. The town, which overhangs these ravines, has a curious aspect, many of the houses appearing perched on crags which have been cut off from the main body.
ETCHELLS, a town of Cheshire, in the p. and m. WSW of Stockport. Pop. 1,476.
ETCHEN, a town of Asia Minor, near the mouth of the Etchenchai, and 15 m. S of Makri.

ETCHENCHAI, a river of Asia Minor, which takes its rise in the mountains which separate the sanjaks of Mentesha and Adalia, runs S, and, after a course of about 63 m., falls into the Mediterranean ESE of Seven capes. It is the Xanthus of the ancients.
ETCHILHAMPTON, a parish in Wilts, 3 m. ESE of Devizes. Area 3,160 acres. Pop. 282.
ETCHINGHAM, a parish in Sussex, 8 m. ESE of Wadhurst. Area 4,240 acres. Pop. 820.
ETCHMIADZIN. See ECHMIADZIN.
ETEN, a coast-town of Peru, in the dep. of Libertad, in S lat. 6° 50'.
ETHERINGTON'S BAY, a bay on the NW coast of the island of St. Vincent, a little to the N of Chateau-Belir bay.

ETHI ROW, a small but important stream in Lancashi e. It rises in a steep valley, among gritstone hit to the E of Manchester, and about 12 m. from its source, unites with the Goyt, which joins the Irwell at Stockport. This stream furnishes a number of mills and works with water to give mo- - Celly a. 11 Rel 1 W. 80

ETHIOPIA, or ÆTHIOPIA, a name applied by ancient geographers to Arabia, and the unknown countries S of Egypt, but ultimately restricted by them to the tropical parts of Africa. In modern geography it is not unfrequently used as a general designation for Nubia, Sennaar, and Abyssinia.
ETICHOUE, a town and commune of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders, 12 m. S of Oudenarde. Pop. 2,806.

ETIENNE (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire.—The arrond comprises an area of 102,487 hect.: and contains 8 cant., viz. Bourg-Argental, Chambon-Feugerolles, Saint-Chamond, Saint-Etienne, Saint-Genît-Malifaux, Saint-Héand, Pélussin, and Rive-de-Gier. Pop. in 1831, 149,189; in 1846, 188,390.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 49,460; in 1841, 72,120.—The town is situated in the vast coal-basin of the same name, on the Furand, 22 m. SE of Montbrison, and 35 m. SW of Lyons, at the junction of the Lyons and St. Etienne Andrezieux, and Roanne railroads. Pop. in 1789, 28,392; in 1821, 19,103; in 1831, 33,064; and in 1841, 48,554. It is built of grey sandstone, and the newer part is laid out with great regularity. It has numerous squares, one of which, near its centre, is adorned with a small obelisk and fountain. Its streets are generally straight and spacious, and the houses good, although gloomy-looking, from the dark colour of the stone of which they are built. The principal buildings are the town-hall, the churches, of which there are five, the college, the theatre, the museum, and the library. E. has also several hospitals, an asylum for the deaf and dumb, and several schools. Its manufactures, for which it is chiefly noted, are various and extensive, consisting of arms of all kinds, iron and copper-ware, cutlery, silk and woollen fabrics, ribbon, tulle, articles in caoutchouc and eaude-Cologne. About 400,000 kilog., or 880,228 lbs., of silk, chiefly of the finest quality, are annually employed in the ribbon-manufactures of this town and of the adjacent districts, producing goods to the value of 32,000,000 francs, and giving employment to 60,000 weavers, and 60 artist painters and designers. There are also numerous iron and copper founderies and steel-manufactories, situated along the banks of the river, the waters of which are noted for their excellence in tempering steel as well as in dyeing.—The surrounding coal-basin is one of the most important in France, producing annually from 25,000,000 to 30,000,000 quintals of coal,—a quantity adequate to supply the entire consumption of the country. Fairs are held 7 times a-year. This town is supposed by some authors to occupy the site of the ancient Furanum of the Romans; but its origin is more generally ascribed to a castle, built by the counts of Forez towards the end of the 10th century. In 1441, in the reign of Charles VII., it was enclosed by a wall the remains of which still exist. It suffered much during the civil wars of the 16th century, and from the ravages of the plague in 1585, and again in 1628-29.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of the Landes, cant. and com. of Saint-Esprit. Pop. 1,250.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. 3 m. NE of Remiremont. Pop. 1,422.

ETIENNE-DE-BAIGORRY (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrenees, arrond. of Mauleon. The town is 26 m. W of Mauleon, and 25 m. SSE of Bayonne, on an affluent of the Nive, at an alt. of 551 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1841, 3,266. Thas a blast furnace and a foundery. In the adja t mountains are mines of lead and iron, and marble quarries. Rock- ETI crystal, topazes, sapphires, and other precious stones, are also found in considerable quantities.

ETIENNE-DU-BOIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Treffort, 8 m. NE of Bourg-en-Bresse. Pop. 1,605.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Palluau, 28 m. NNE of Les Sables. Pop. 1,961.
ETIENNE-EN-BRESSE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Moutret, 12 m. NW of Louchans. Pop. 1,055.
ETIENNE-DE-CHALARONNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Thoissey, 24 m. N of Trévoux. Pop. 1,484.
ETIENNE-CHOMELL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Riom-es- Montagne, 18 m. NW of Mauriac. Pop. 1,133.
ETIENNE-EN-COGLES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Saint-Brice-en-Cogles, 8 m. NW of Fougeres. Pop. 1,937.
ETIENNE-DE-CORCOU'E (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Segé, 27 m. S of Nantes. Pop. 1,205.
ETIENNE-DE-CROISSEY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Voiron, 24 m. NNW of Grenoble. Pop. 1,590.

ETIENNE-DE-DE VOLUY (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes- Alpes, arrond. of Gap. The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 2,139; in 1841, 2,114. The village is 13 m. NW of Gap, on the r. bank of the Soulouaze. Pop. 730. It has an annual fair.
ETIENNE-DE-DROUX (SAINT). See SAINT ESTE PHE.
ETIENNE-DE-FONTBELLON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Aubenas, 24 m. NW of Privas. Pop. 1,286.
ETIENNE-DE-FURSAC (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. of Grand- Bourg, 27 m. W of Guéret. Pop. 1,845.
ETIENNE-LALLIER (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Saint- Georges-du-Vièvre, 6 m. S of Pont-Audemer. Pop 1,168.
ETIENNE-LARDEYROL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. of Saint-Julien-Chapteuil, 6 m. WNW of Le Puy. Pop. 1,026.

ETIENNE-DE-LUGDARE'S (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ardeche, arrond. of Largentiere. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,508; in 1841, 4,577. The town is 21 m. NW of Largentiere, on the r. bank of the Mamejan. Pop. 1,892.
ETIENNE-DE-MER-MORTE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Machecoul, 27 m. S of Nantes. Pop. 1,040.

ETIENNE-DE-MONT-LUC (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire- Inferieure, arrond. of Savenay. The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,444; in 1841, 14,659. The village is 12 m. SE of Savenay, and 12 m. NW of Nantes. Pop. in 1841, 4,540. It has two annual fairs. Porcelain clay is found in the vicinity.

ETIENNE-LES-ORGUES (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Alpes, arrond. of Forcalquier. The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,825; in 1841, 3,978. The village is 10 m. N of Forcalquier, at the foot of Mont Lure. Pop. 1.208.
ÉTIENNE-DI-RENNEVILLE (SAINT). See LA COMMANDERIE.
ETIENNE-DU-ROUVRAY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant of Grand Couronne, 4 m. S of Rouen, near the r. bank of the Seine. Pop. 1,841. It has quarries of marble.

ETIENNE-DE-SAINT-GEOIRS (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Isère, arrond. of Saint-Marcellin. The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,390; in 1841, 12,522. The town is 14 m. N of Saint-Marcellin, and 23 m. NW of Grenoble, near the r. bank of the Rinal. Pop. 1,944.
ETIENNE-SUR-USSON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Sauxillanges, 9 m. ESE of Issoire. Pop. 1,124.
ETIENNE-DU-VALDONNES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lozère, cant. and 3 m. SW of Mende. Pop. 1,140.
ETIENNE-VALLEE-FRANCAISE (SAINT), or
SAINT-ETIENNE-DI-VALFRANCESQUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lozere, cant. of Saint- Germain-de-Calberte, 9 m. SE of Florac. Pop. 1,901.
ETIENNE-LA-VARENNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Belleville-sur-Saône, 9 m. SW of Villefranche-sur-Saône. Pop. 1,499. It produces good wine.
ETIMADPUR, a town of moderate size, in the prov. of Lahore, in N. India, 14 m. from Firozabad.
ETINAL, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Raon-l'Etape. Pop. 1,856.

ETIVE (LOCH), a navigable inlet of the sea, in Argyleshire, nearly 20 m. long, but of very unequal breadth. Its shores are indented with creeks and bays, which afford safe anchorage in any wind. The extremity of Loch E. bends its course, from Bunawe ferry, in a NE direction, till it terminates in a point, where it receives the waters of the Etive river, running through Glen-Etive. About 7 m. from its entrance from the sea, it contracts into a narrow channel.
ETKO. See EDKU.
ETLINGEN. See ETTLINGEN.
ETLIP. See EDLIP.
ETMASER, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 36 m. N of Shamir.
ETNA. See ÆTNA.
ETOGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. and 12 m. S of Epernay. Pop. 587.

ETOILE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Valence. Pop. 1,841.—Also a com. in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Lons-le-Saulmer. Pop. 687.—Also a com. in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Picquigny. Pop. 822.
ETOILE (L'), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Neuilly. Pop. 450.

ETON, a parish and town in the co. of Buckingham, 1 m. N of Windsor, and 23 m. W by S of London. Area of p. 690 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,026; in 1831, 3,232; in 1841, 3,526.—The town of E. is situated in a fertile valley on the N side of the Thames, opposite to Windsor, with which it is connected by a neat iron-bridge. It consists principally of one narrow street, which is well paved and lighted, and contains some good houses, in which the 'oppidans' or boys not on the foundation lodge. It has long been celebrated, or, as Lambard has it, been "notorious for a scole which King Henry VI. buylded, as he did the kinge's college of Cambridge, wherto, by order of their foundation, this scole sendeth her ripe fruite." The school, as now taught, is divided into upper and lower; and these are subdivided into three classes. Besides the 70 scholars on the foundation, there are a number of stipendiary pupils, generally 400 or 500, the sons of noblemen and wealthy commoners. The foundation-scholars are entitled to fellowships and scholarships in King's college, Cambridge, for which there is an annual election. They succeed according to seniority; on three years' resi- | Pop. 1,289. ETR dence at college obtain a fellowship; and are superannuated at 19. The fellows of King's college, Cambridge, are altogether elected from the foundationboys of E. Almost all the prime ministers of the last century were 'Eton men;' and at a later period E. can boast of having produced the Duke of Wellington, Mr. Canning, Lord Wellesley, Lord Grenville, Lord Grey, and that fine scholar and philosophical writer, Mr. Hallam. E. school consists of two courts or quadrangles, communicating by an ancient gateway. In the centre of the outer court is a bronze statue of Henry VI. The chapel, formerly called Christopher-hall, is an elegant Gothic structure, resembling, in its style and decorations, the chapel of King's college, Cambridge. The inner court contains apartments for the provost, and dormitories for the fellows, and the splendid college library. The 'playing-fields' are very extensive; and the whole is beautifully situated on the banks of the Thames, with the Little park and Windsor castle on the opposite side.
ETOUTTEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Yerville, 4 m. N of Yvetot. Pop. 1,040.
ETOUX (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Beaujeu, 15 m. NNW of Villefranche-sur-Saône. Pop. 1,228. It produces good wine.
ETRE'AUPONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of La Chapelle, 6 m. N of Vervins. Pop. 1,409.
ETRE'CHY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, cant. and 8 m. N of Etampes, on the railroad from Paris to Orleans. Pop. 1,171.
ETREILLERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Vermand, 10 m. W of Saint- Quentin. Pop. 1,237.
ETREILLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Argentré, 6 m. S of Vitré. Pop. 1,903.

ETREPAGNY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, arrond. of Andelys. The cant. comprises 24 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,069; in 1841, 9,733. The village is 15 m. NE of Les Andelys. Pop. 1,605. It has a cotton spinning-mill and manufactories of lace.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Dampierre. Pop. 799. Pottery and brick form the chief articles of local manufacture.
ETRETAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Criquetot-Lesnevalsur-le-Manche, 19 m. NNE of Le Havre. Pop. 1,518. Soda from sea-weed is extensively manufactured here.
ETREUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Wassigny, 19 m. NW of Vervins. Pop. in 1841, 1,719. It has a manufactory of chicory-coffee.
ETREVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Routot, 8 m. ENE of Pont- Audemer. Pop. 1,155.
ETRICHE', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Durtal, 21 m. NW of Baugé. Pop. 1,198.

ETRIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Loire, cant. of Sennecy, 15 m. S of Châlon-sur-Saône. Pop. 1,406. ETRŒUNGT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. and 4 m. S of Avesnes. Pop. 1,987. It has a cotton spinning-mill, and manufactories of sugar from beet-root, cheese, articles in wood, flannel, and hosiery.
ETROUSSAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Allier, cant. of Chantille, 12 m. N of Gannat. \

ETRURIA, a hamlet in the p. of Stoke-upon- Trent, co. of Stafford, 12 m. NE of Newcastle-under- Lyne, intersected by the Manchester and Birmingham railway and the Grand Trunk canal. This village, remarkable for the beauty of its situation and the arrangement of its buildings, is celebrated as having been the seat of Josiah Wedgewood, Esq., the original inventor of the beautiful description of ware which bears his name, and who died here in 1795. The village itself obtained its name from Mr. Wedgewood's excellent imitations of Etruscan vases. To the same ingenious gentleman's experiments, we are indebted for the invention of several other species of earthenware and porcelain, adapted to various purposes of ornament and use. These expanded by the industry and ingenuity of the different manufacturers into an infinity of forms, for ornament and use, and variously painted and embellished, constitute nearly the whole of the present fine English earthenwares and porcelain, which, considered as objects of national art, industry, and commerce, may be ranked among the most important manufactures in the kingdom.
ETSCH. See Adige, and Botzen.
ETSDORF, a village of Lower Austria, in the circle of Korneuburg. Pop. 615.
ETTELBRUCK, a commune and village of Luxemburg, on the Alzette, 8 m. SW of Diekirch. Pop. 3,504.
ETTELGHEM, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of W. Flanders. Pop. 690.
ETTEN, a parish and village of Holland, in N. Brabant, 8 m. WSW of Breda. Pop. 5,391.
ETTENBACH, a small river of Baden, in the Brisgau, which flows into the Rhine.
ETTENBEUREN, a large village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, district of Wettenhausen.

ETTENHEIM, a town of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, 18 m. N of Friburg, on the railway to Carlsruhe. Pop. 3,123. It has considerable linen manufactories, and flax is extensively grown in the environs. About 23 m. to the SE of the town is the once rich, but now secularised, Benedictine abbey of Ettenheim-Münster; which was made over, with the adjacent country, to the grand duke of Baden in 1803.
ETTERSBURG, a hamlet in the grand duchy of Saxe-Weimar, 7 m. from Weimar, on the Ettersberg, a mountain on the skirts of the Thüringer-walde, which has an alt. of 1,431 ft.
EITERWINDEN, a village of Saxe-Weimar, on the Elte, S of Eisenach. Pop. 259.
ETTINGEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basle, circle of Birseck. Pop. 500.
ETTISWYL, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, bail. of Willisau. Pop. 876.

ETTLINGEN, a town of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, on the Alb, 4½ m. S of Carlsruhe. Pop. 4,250. It has cotton spinning-mills, powdermills, paper-mills, manufactories of beet-root sugar, and an active trade.
ETTON, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NW of Beverley. Area 3,960 acres. Pop. 425 .- Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 6 m. NNE of Peterborough. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 118.

ETTRICK, a parish in the SW of Selkirkshire. Diagonally, from Cadgercraig on the NE to Mickle-
whin-fell on the SW, it measures 12½ m.; and from Merecleugh on the NW to Moodlaw loch on the SE, 10 m.; and it contains an area of 43,968 imperial acres, or 68.69 sq. m. Its surface is a sea of hills, beautiful and varied in appearance, and everywhere wearing the mantle of romance. Toward the sources of the streams, along the W and S boundaries, the summits tower aloft to a considerable elevation. Old Ettrick hill is 1,860 ft. above the level of the sea; EUC Wardlaw or Weirdlaw hill, 1,986 ft.; and Ettrickpen, 2,200 ft. The p. from its extreme SW angle to the middle of its NE boundary, is cut into two nearly equal parts by the Ettrick; and is traversed southward in its S section by Tima-water and Rankleburn, and eastward in its N section by Tushielawburn. On the NW verge is the Loch-of-Lowes, less than a mile in length, fed by five mountain-rills, and particularly by the incipient stream of Yarrow. Communicating with the Loch-of-Lowes, lying within a furlong S of it, and stretching away from the boundary-line into the conterminous parish of Yarrow, is the beautiful lake called St. Mary's Loch. -On Ettrick-water, almost at the centre of the parish, stands the little hamlet of E. One of the very few houses, near the lonely church and its little straggling retinue of trees, was the birth-place of Hogg 'the Ettrick Shepherd.' In the sequestered cemetery is a fine monument, of recent erection, over the ashes of Thomas Boston, the well-known author of 'The Fourfold State.' Pop. of the p. in 1801, 445; in 1831, 530; in 1841, 525.

ETTRICK, a river of Selkirkshire, rising in the extreme SW angle of the co.; and, with few sinuosities, pursuing a NE direction over its whole course. The source of its highest head-water is on the S side of the central summits of the highest mountainrange of the southern Highlands, between Loch-fell and Capel-fell. It falls into the Tweed 2 m. below the town of Selkirk. Its entire course is about 28 m.
ETTRINGEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, on the Wertach, N of Turkheim. Pop. 643.

ETWALL, a parish in the co. and 6 m. WSW of Derby. Area 3,680 acres. Pop. 689. EU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Dieppe. The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,458; in 1841, 15,685.—The town is pleasantly situated in a valley, on the Bresle, 2 m. above the entrance of that river into the British channel, and 22 m. NE of Dieppe. Pop. in 1841, 3,977; in 1846, 3,749. It is well built, and has a fine square. The parish church, a fine Gothic edifice, is remarkable for a subterraneous chapel, and in the college-church are the magnificent tombs of the Guises. This town has manufactories of lace, serge, linseed oil, flax, and soap, spinning-mills, extensive lime, brick, tile and gypsum kilns, bleacheries, wax-works, and saw-mills. It is an entrepot for the grain of the Somme, and has also a considerable trade in hemp, flax, linen, and timber. Its port, Treport, is situated at the mouth of the Bresle.—E. is a very ancient town, and is supposed to be the Aga of the Romans. It belonged to the dukes of Penthièvre in 1445, and was burned by order of Louis XI. to prevent its falling into the hands of the English.—The forest of Eu comprises an area 21 m. in length, and nearly 8 m. in breadth. In the vicinity, surrounded by an extensive park and gardens, is the magnificent Chateau d'Eu, containing the finest collection of historical portraits in France; and noted for the interview which took place there in 1843, between Queen Victoria and the late king of the French, Louis Philippe, who inherited the Comté-d'Eu from his mother, daughter of the Duc de Penthievre.
EUARTH, a township in the p. of Llanfair-Dyffryn-Clwyd, Denbighshire. Pop. 304.
EUCHAN WATER, a rivulet in Dumfries-shire, which rises in Black-Larg hill, on the confines of Ayrshire, and after a course of 81 m., falls into the Nith near Sanguhar.
EUCHAR, a rivulet in Argyleshire, which issues from Loch Scammodale, in the district of Kilninver, and falls into the sound of Mull.
EUCLID, a township of Cuyahoga co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 155 m. NNE of Columbus, on Lake Erie. Pop. in 1840, 1,775.
EUCHTERSHEIM, or Eichtersheim, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, SE of Wiesloch. Pop. 892.
EUDOKEEF, or FOGGY ISLANDS, a group of small islands in the North Pacific, to the E of the peninsula of Alashka, in N lat. 56° 31'.
EUERNDORF, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, 29 m. N of Würzburg, on the 1. bank of the Saale. Pop. 892. The cultivation of the vine forms the chief branch of local industry.

EUFEMIA (SANTA), a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 44 m. N of Cordova, partido of Kinojosa-del-Duque.—Also a town in Leon, in the prov. and 32 m. NW of Valladolid, partido of Medina-de-Rioseca. — Also a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 3 m. SE of Brescia, on the road to Verona. Pop. 1,360. It has several spinning and saw-mills.—Also a village of Austria, in Dalmatia, circle of Zara, on the island of Ugliano. -Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria- Ultra, district and 9 m. SSE of Palmi.—Also a town in the same prov., in the district and 10 m. W of Nicastro, on the gulf of Santa Eufemia. Pop. 1,100. It occupies the site of the ancient Terina. In 1638 it was destroyed by an earthquake. Wine is cultivated in the environs.

EUGANEI (MONTI), a range of hills in Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice and delegation of Padua. They extend a distance of about 12 m. in a SE direction between the Bisato, which separates them on the NW from the Berici mountains, the Bacchiglione, and the canal of Montselice. Their highest summit, Monte Venda, does not exceed 1,900 ft. in height above sea-level, and they are generally well-wooded and cultivated. They contain large quantities of fossil fish, beds of fine marble, fuller's earth, and potter's clay. They have also numerous thermal springs and baths.
EUGELAND, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of South Brabant, cant. of Uccle. Pop. 371.
EUGELBEEK, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of South Brabant, cant. of Schaffen. Pop. 107.
EUGENE, a village of Vermillion co., in the state of India, U. S., 85 m. W of Indianapolis, on the S side of Big-Vermillion river, 13 m. from the confluence of the Wabash. Pop. in 1840, 1,048.
